ℹ️ These are system logs for troubleshooting. Some errors and failures are normal during operation. These logs are most useful for developers.
Please select a container to view logs
ℹ️ Remote Logging: When enabled, system logs will be sent to a remote logging system. This is useful for the developer and for troubleshooting issues. Logs may include system diagnostics, error messages, and operational data. Changes require a server restart to take effect.
ℹ️ These are HTTP request logs showing all web traffic to the server. Useful for monitoring access patterns and troubleshooting connection issues.

📡 SDR Frontend Status

ℹ️ About A/D Overranges: Overranges are expected during automatic gain control (AGC) operation. The AGC uses hysteresis—allowing some overranges before adjusting gain—to learn optimal levels. What matters is that overranges remain under control, not that they're zero.

🏋️ Benchmark ▶

The benchmark tool launches in a terminal window and simulates concurrent listeners to stress-test this system. It supports up to 1,000 simulated users (2,000 channels) and measures how the system performs under load.

⚠️ Start low and work up. Begin with a small number of users and gradually increase, confirming the system remains stable at each step before going higher. Monitor CPU, memory, and audio quality during each run before increasing the load further.

💡 It is advised to have the web UI open and tuned to a known clean signal during each run. Watch the spectrum display and listen for any audio stalling or jittering — these are the first signs the system is approaching its limit.

⚡ CPU Governor ▶

The CPU governor controls how the processor scales its clock speed. Linux defaults to powersave or ondemand, which throttle the CPU and ramp up slowly under load — on some systems this may contribute to audio stuttering or missed decoder cycles. The performance governor requests maximum clock speed at all times, which may improve real-time streaming stability on affected systems, but will typically increase power consumption and heat output.

⚙️ radiod CPU Pinning ▶

Dedicating one or more physical CPU cores exclusively to radiod can reduce scheduling jitter and improve real-time IQ processing stability. This tool analyses your CPU topology, measures idle time, and suggests the best core(s) to pin — then optionally writes the cpuset into your docker-compose.yml and restarts UberSDR.

Detailed report of CPU topology, thread scheduling, IRQ affinity, kernel isolation parameters, and radiod CPU usage — useful for diagnosing real-time performance issues.

Loading system statistics...

🎨 Web UI Defaults

These settings control the default appearance for new visitors who have not yet set their own preferences. Individual users can override any of these via the UI controls, and their personal preference is saved in their browser. The server default only applies when a user has no saved preference for that setting.

✅ No server restart required. Changes take effect immediately for new page loads — existing users are not affected.

📊 Default Signal Bar Meter Mode

Controls the display mode of the signal bar meter (under the waterfall) for new visitors. Users can click the meter to cycle through modes.

🎯 Default S-Meter Mode

Controls which mode the needle S-meter (canvas) starts in for new visitors. Users can click the S-meter canvas to cycle through modes.

When enabled, the two mini dBFS/SNR history charts are shown to the right of the S-meter for new visitors. Users can toggle them with the ◀ button on the S-meter.

🌈 Default Waterfall Palette

The colour palette used for the waterfall display for new visitors.

🔆 Default Auto-Contrast

The symmetric dB offset applied to the auto-range window for new visitors. The noise floor is raised by +N dB and the ceiling is lowered by -N dB, making signals pop out against the noise. Range: 0–20. Default: 10. (Matches the Contrast slider in the spectrum controls.)

0 — Wider dB window 20 — Tighter dB window

📊 Default VU Meter Style

Controls the audio VU meter display style for new visitors. Users can click the VU meter to toggle between styles.

⚙️ Default Waterfall Options

Controls which waterfall features are enabled by default for new visitors. Users can toggle these via the checkboxes in the spectrum controls.

📱 Mobile Settings

Settings that apply to visitors on narrow/mobile screens (≤1024 px wide). Users can always override these with the controls on the page — the server default only applies when no preference has been saved.

Controls whether new mobile visitors see the tuning Buttons or the drag Wheel in the frequency console. Users can switch at any time using the Btns/Wheel radio toggle.

🔊 Default Audio Buffer

Sets the default maximum audio buffer size for new visitors before frames are dropped. Lower values reduce latency but may cause stuttering on slower connections. Users can change this at any time via the Audio Settings modal (click the buffer indicator).

🎨 Theme Colours

Customise the colour palette used throughout the receiver UI. Defaults reproduce the original appearance exactly — change any colour to theme the entire interface. These are server-wide settings; users cannot override them individually.

🔤 Control Text Colour

Colour of control labels on light backgrounds — Volume, Left, Right, Low, High, Scroll, Contrast, etc. Defaults to dark grey (#495057) which matches the original appearance. Server-wide setting; users cannot override individually.

🎨 Default Bandwidth Indicator Colour

The colour of the bandwidth indicator bar drawn on the waterfall showing the tuned receive bandwidth. Users can right-click the bar to change the colour; their personal choice is saved in their browser and always takes priority.

🖼️ Spectrum Background Image

Upload a custom PNG image to display behind the spectrum line graph (split view). The image is stretched to fill the 220 px spectrum drawing area below the frequency scale. PNG only · max 500 KB.

Current image:

No background image set.

0.00 — Invisible 1.00 — Fully opaque

🎨 Band Colour Intensity

Controls how vivid the amateur band overlays appear in the bookmark area of the spectrum. 0.50 is the default pastel appearance; 1.00 gives bold, fully saturated colours. This is a server-wide setting; users cannot override it individually.

0.50 — Default pastel 1.00 — Vivid

📡 Station ID Overlay

Show the station callsign, name, and location in the top-right corner of the spectrum line graph. The first line shows <callsign> – <name> and the second line shows <location> in smaller text. Values come from the Station Info section of the main config. This is a server-wide setting; users cannot override it individually.

Overlay text colour
A 1 px black drop-shadow is always added for legibility over any background.

⚠️ Configuration Warnings

⚠️ Force Update

WARNING: This will trigger an immediate system update!

The system will:

  • Pull the latest Docker images
  • Restart all services
  • Disconnect all active users

Note: The update process may take a couple of minutes. You will need to log back in after the update completes.

🖥️ Install CW Skimmer

⚠️ CW Skimmer requires significant CPU resources.

CW Skimmer performs real-time DSP decoding across the entire HF spectrum simultaneously. This is computationally intensive, especially when combined with WSJT-X digital mode decoding (FT8/FT4/WSPR).

Recommended minimum: An 8-core modern CPU (e.g. Intel Core i7/i9, AMD Ryzen 7/9, or equivalent).

If your system does not meet this requirement, running CW Skimmer may cause audio dropouts, missed spots, or system instability.

Are you confident your UberSDR instance has enough processing power to continue?
